text: A standard posterior lateral thoracotomy is performed.
The intercostal muscle above the level of the incision is mobilised and reflected back in order to protect the intercostal nerve and reduce post-operative pain.
This is seen a bit more clearly later in the video.
In this case, there was evidence of emphysematous lung at the apex, which was densely adherent to the chest wall.
Partial decortication was necessary in order to mobilise the lung sufficiently to perform the operation.
Lesions were identified by palpation and then the laser was set up ready to perform the metastatectomy.
Here you can see the laser in action.
Initially we marked the lesions using the diathermy to give us a rough idea about where the lesion sits.
You'll see this a bit later on in the video.
The advantage of a dual wave laser system is that the relative contributions of the two wavelengths can be blended in order to achieve different desired effects, for example incision or coagulation.
Here you can see after the first lesion is removed, the laser is set to a coagulation blend allowing for haemostasis.
Once adequate haemostasis is achieved, the laser blend is switched back to incision and the attention is turned to the next nodule.
As well as the wavelength blend, the power of the laser can be altered as well.
Combined with altering the distance from the tip of the laser to the tissue, these two methods can be used to alter the amount of energy that's transferred to the lung tissue.
Here you can see that once the lesion is palpated, it can be useful to mark it with a diathermy to ensure the boundaries of the lesion remain visible.
You can see the operation of the laser is reasonably straightforward.
It's activated by using a pedal and steady controlled movements of the laser tip are used to negotiate the margins of the lesion.
This particular laser has an inbuilt vacuum system which extracts the smoke as you can see here.
The fourth lesion has just been removed and a final sequence of coagulation is performed.
Our routine practice is to over-sew the excision beds using a 3-0 PDS suture.
This helps to achieve pneumostasis and haemostasis, particularly in the setting of multiple lesion excisions.
A double layer of sutures is performed.
The laser has a number of adaptations which allow it to be used in VAT surgery and also in airway surgery.
An open approach was used in this case due to the number of lesions and a desire to minimize the operative time.
The other advantage of using the laser is that it minimizes lung tissue loss.
The original plan for this patient was for multiple wedge resections and a segmentectomy.
And where resection of multiple nodules is required, the use of the laser can significantly reduce the volume of lung tissue lost.
Here some additional haemostasis is performed at the site of the earlier partial decortication at the apex.
A pleural catheter for continuous local anaesthetic infiltration is cited to ensure adequate post-operative analgesia.
A thorough washout with warm water is performed and a test inflation reveals a small air leak at the site of the decortication.
A troublesome area is oversown with another PDS and a subsequent test inflation appears to be satisfactory.
28 French chest tube is sighted apically.
Here you can see how we anchor the pleura of the previously mobilised intercostal muscle flap which lifts it into the intercostal space.
Care should be taken when performing the pericostal stitch to avoid strangulation of the inferior intercostal nerve bundle and this can be achieved by skimming the inferior border of the lower rib.
The remaining muscle layers are closed in a standard fashion using an absorbable vicral stitch.
The skin is finished off with an absorbable 3A monocryl stitch.
